question_text: Drip irrigation is most accurately described as which specific method of applying water directly to crop plants?
explanation: Drip (trickle) irrigation delivers water slowly at low pressure through emitters placed near each plant's root zone. Water application rates typically range from 2 to 8 litres per hour per emitter. This method achieves the highest application efficiency of 85-95 percent because it minimises evaporation, runoff and deep percolation losses compared to surface methods.
topic: Irrigation Practices, Drainage and Water Management
subject: agriculture

options.text: Flooding the entire field surface with a sheet of water
Slow delivery of water through emitters near the root zone
Spraying water as artificial rain through rotating nozzles
Running water in open furrows between crop ridges

elimination_hints.hint: Flooding is surface irrigation, not drip
Spraying through nozzles is sprinkler irrigation
Open furrows are furrow irrigation

question_text: Sprinkler irrigation applies water to the crop field by which specific mechanical mechanism involving pressurised water?
explanation: Sprinkler irrigation sprays water through nozzles under pressure to simulate natural rainfall over the crop canopy. Water is pumped through a pipe network and discharged through sprinkler heads that rotate or oscillate to give uniform coverage. Efficiency is 65-75 percent, better than surface methods but lower than drip, because some water is lost to wind drift and evaporation.
topic: Irrigation Practices, Drainage and Water Management
subject: agriculture

options.text: Dripping water slowly through emitters near each plant root
Flooding the field by opening canal outlets to basins
Spraying pressurised water through nozzles simulating rainfall
Running water along the soil surface in narrow furrows

elimination_hints.hint: Emitter-based delivery is drip, not sprinkler
Canal-based flooding is surface irrigation
Narrow furrows are furrow irrigation

question_text: The two major sources of irrigation water for Indian agriculture are most accurately classified into which two broad categories?
explanation: Indian agriculture is irrigated from two broad sources: surface water (canals, rivers, tanks, reservoirs) and groundwater (tube wells, bore wells, open wells). According to census data, groundwater through wells and tube wells irrigates about 60 percent of India's net irrigated area, while canal irrigation covers about 25 percent, and tanks and other sources the rest.
topic: Irrigation Practices, Drainage and Water Management
subject: agriculture

options.text: Seawater desalination and rainwater harvesting only
Surface water (canals, tanks) and groundwater (wells, tube wells)
Imported bottled water and treated sewage water only
Glacier meltwater and atmospheric moisture extraction only

elimination_hints.hint: Desalination contributes negligibly to Indian irrigation
Bottled water is not an irrigation source
These are marginal, not major irrigation sources in India

question_text: Furrow irrigation is best suited for which specific category of field crops grown in raised rows in India?
explanation: Furrow irrigation runs water along small channels (furrows) between raised ridges on which row crops like sugarcane, maize, cotton, vegetables and potatoes are planted. Water infiltrates laterally from the furrow into the root zone of the ridges. It is more efficient than flat flooding because only a portion of the field surface is wetted.
topic: Irrigation Practices, Drainage and Water Management
subject: agriculture

options.text: Submerged paddy rice requiring continuous standing water
Dense pasture grasses sown by broadcasting seed randomly
Row crops like sugarcane, maize and cotton on raised ridges
Orchard trees with wide spacing of 8 metres or more

elimination_hints.hint: Paddy uses basin flooding, not furrow
Dense pastures are irrigated by border strips, not furrows
Wide-spaced orchards are best with drip, not furrow
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing orchard with row crop irrigation

question_text: Irrigation efficiency is most correctly defined as which specific ratio expressing how effectively water is used by crops?
explanation: Irrigation efficiency is the ratio of water actually stored in the crop root zone and used beneficially by plants to the total water delivered to the field, expressed as a percentage. Losses occur through deep percolation, surface runoff, evaporation from soil and canal seepage. Higher efficiency means less water wasted per unit of crop water need satisfied.
topic: Irrigation Practices, Drainage and Water Management
subject: agriculture

options.text: Total water pumped divided by the area of the farm
Water beneficially used by crop divided by total water delivered
Total crop yield divided by total amount of rainfall received
Total canal length divided by the command area of the canal

elimination_hints.hint: Water pumped per area is not an efficiency ratio
Yield per rainfall is water productivity, not irrigation efficiency
Canal length per area is canal density, not efficiency

question_text: The IW/CPE ratio method of scheduling irrigation in wheat uses which two specific measurements to time each watering?
explanation: The IW/CPE ratio method schedules irrigation based on Irrigation Water depth (IW, typically 5 or 6 cm per irrigation) divided by Cumulative Pan Evaporation (CPE) measured from a USWB class-A open-pan evaporimeter. When cumulative pan evaporation reaches a preset threshold (e.g. IW/CPE = 0.8 or 1.0), irrigation is applied. This ensures water is given based on actual crop water demand.
topic: Irrigation Practices, Drainage and Water Management
subject: agriculture

options.text: Soil temperature and relative humidity of the air simultaneously
Crop height and number of leaves per plant on specific days
Irrigation water depth and cumulative pan evaporation readings
Total rainfall and total canal inflow to the command area

elimination_hints.hint: Temperature and humidity are weather factors, not the IW/CPE components
Morphological measures are not the IW/CPE components
Rainfall and canal flow are supply measures, not IW/CPE

question_text: Waterlogging in crop fields occurs when which specific unfavourable soil water condition persists for a prolonged period?
explanation: Waterlogging occurs when the water table rises to or near the root zone and all soil pores remain saturated for a prolonged period, depriving roots of oxygen. Causes include over-irrigation, poor drainage, canal seepage and flat topography. Waterlogged crops show yellowing, wilting despite wet soil, root decay and yield decline. Drainage by surface channels or sub-surface pipes is the standard remedy.
topic: Irrigation Practices, Drainage and Water Management
subject: agriculture

options.text: When the soil becomes extremely dry and cracked in summer
When the water table rises and all soil pores remain saturated
When only the surface layer is wetted by light sprinkler rain
When drip emitters apply water only to a small root zone area

elimination_hints.hint: Extreme dryness is drought, the opposite of waterlogging
Light wetting is not prolonged saturation of the root zone
Targeted drip does not cause waterlogging

question_text: Duty of canal water in irrigation engineering is most correctly defined as which specific relationship between water and land area?
explanation: Duty is defined as the area of land (in hectares) that can be irrigated by one cumec (cubic metre per second) of continuous canal flow throughout the base period of a crop. A high duty value means the water is being used efficiently over a larger area. Duty depends on crop type, climate, soil type and conveyance losses.
topic: Irrigation Practices, Drainage and Water Management
subject: agriculture

options.text: Total length of the canal network per unit command area
Number of hours the canal gate is kept open daily
Area of land irrigated by one cumec of canal flow through the base period
Total rainfall received in the command area in one season

elimination_hints.hint: Canal length per area is canal density, not duty
Gate operating hours measure rotation, not duty
Rainfall is a separate water source, not duty

question_text: The Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana (PMKSY) of India was launched with which overarching motto for irrigation development?
explanation: PMKSY was launched in 2015 with the motto 'Har Khet Ko Paani' (Water to Every Field) and 'Per Drop More Crop'. It integrates existing schemes for end-to-end irrigation solutions including water source creation, distribution, farm-level application and micro-irrigation promotion. The scheme aims to expand cultivable area under assured irrigation and improve water-use efficiency.
topic: Irrigation Practices, Drainage and Water Management
subject: agriculture

options.text: Export every drop of water to foreign countries for revenue
Har Khet Ko Paani (Water to Every Field) and Per Drop More Crop
Ban all groundwater extraction across the entire country permanently
Replace all canal irrigation with only rainfed farming in India

elimination_hints.hint: India aims to use water domestically, not export it
PMKSY promotes efficient use, not blanket bans
PMKSY expands irrigation, not replaces it

question_text: In a field where drip irrigation operates at 90 percent efficiency and flood irrigation at 45 percent, how much water does drip save per 1000 litres of crop water requirement?
explanation: To supply 1000 litres of crop need at 45 percent efficiency, flood requires 1000 / 0.45 = 2222 litres of water delivered. At 90 percent efficiency, drip requires 1000 / 0.90 = 1111 litres. The saving is 2222 minus 1111 = 1111 litres, which is about 50 percent of the flood water requirement, demonstrating the drip advantage.
topic: Irrigation Practices, Drainage and Water Management
subject: agriculture

options.text: About 250 litres saved per 1000 litres crop need
About 500 litres saved per 1000 litres crop need
About 1111 litres saved per 1000 litres crop need
No water saved because both methods deliver the same volume

elimination_hints.hint: 250 litres underestimates the large efficiency gap
500 litres is less than the actual difference of 1111
90% vs 45% efficiency creates a massive volume difference

question_text: Match the irrigation methods in Column A with their typical application efficiency ranges listed in Column B.
explanation: Flood irrigation has 40-50 percent efficiency due to heavy losses; furrow is slightly better at 50-60 percent; sprinkler achieves 65-75 percent by simulating rain; and drip achieves 85-95 percent by delivering water directly to roots. These values guide choice of method for water-scarce situations.
topic: Irrigation Practices, Drainage and Water Management
subject: agriculture

elimination_hints.hint: Flood cannot have the highest efficiency
Flood is not 65-75 percent efficient
Drip cannot have 65-75%; it exceeds 85%

question_text: Match the following irrigation water-loss types in Column A with the point in the system where they mainly occur in Column B.
explanation: Seepage loss occurs through unlined canal beds during conveyance; evaporation loss occurs from open water surfaces and wet soil; deep percolation loss occurs below the root zone due to over-irrigation; and surface runoff loss occurs when water flows off the tail-end of the field. Each loss must be managed differently.
topic: Irrigation Practices, Drainage and Water Management
subject: agriculture

column_b.text: Water flowing off the tail-end of the irrigated field
Water moving below the crop root zone into subsoil
Water vapour lost from open water surfaces and wet soil
Water soaking through unlined canal bed into ground

elimination_hints.hint: Seepage is from canals, not from field tail-end
Seepage is not evaporation
Seepage is not deep percolation from root zone
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing canal seepage with field percolation

question_text: Match the irrigation sources in Column A with their correct descriptions as used in Indian agriculture in Column B.
explanation: Canals distribute water from dams and barrages through a network of channels; tube wells extract groundwater through bore-holes fitted with submersible pumps; tanks are small reservoirs that collect rainwater; and check dams are small barriers across streams for groundwater recharge and small-scale irrigation.
topic: Irrigation Practices, Drainage and Water Management
subject: agriculture

column_b.text: Small barrier across stream for recharge and local irrigation
Small reservoir collecting rainwater for seasonal irrigation
Borehole with submersible pump extracting groundwater
Network of channels distributing dam water to fields

elimination_hints.hint: Canal is not a small barrier across a stream
Canal is not a rainwater-collecting reservoir
Canal is not a borehole with pump

question_text: Read the following assertion and reason about drip irrigation in orchards and choose the most correct option below.
explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Drip delivers water drop by drop directly at the root zone through emitters, wetting only a small fraction of the soil surface and reducing evaporation, runoff and deep percolation losses. This precision placement gives efficiency of 85-95 percent, the highest among all irrigation methods commercially available.
topic: Irrigation Practices, Drainage and Water Management
subject: agriculture
assertion: Drip irrigation achieves the highest water application efficiency of 85 to 95 percent among all methods.
reason: Drip delivers water directly to the root zone through emitters, minimising evaporation and runoff losses.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: Root-zone delivery is precisely why drip is most efficient
Root-zone delivery by emitters is well established
85-95% efficiency for drip is well documented

question_text: Read the assertion and reason about waterlogging in canal-irrigated areas of India and choose the most appropriate option.
explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Continuous seepage from unlined canals and over-irrigation raise the water table progressively until it reaches the root zone, saturating all soil pores and depriving roots of oxygen. Waterlogging also brings dissolved salts to the surface by capillary rise, causing secondary salinisation of fields.
topic: Irrigation Practices, Drainage and Water Management
subject: agriculture
assertion: Canal-irrigated areas in India often suffer from waterlogging and secondary salinisation of soils.
reason: Seepage from unlined canals and over-irrigation progressively raise the water table to the root zone.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: Rising water table is the very cause of waterlogging
Canal seepage raising water table is well established
Waterlogging in canal areas is well documented in India

question_text: Read the assertion and reason about groundwater over-extraction in India and choose the option which best fits.
explanation: The assertion is true but the reason is false. Groundwater over-extraction in India is a serious problem, not because groundwater is unlimited, but precisely because it is a finite and rechargeable resource that is being pumped faster than natural recharge. In many blocks of Punjab, Haryana and Rajasthan the water table is falling by 1-3 metres per year, threatening long-term irrigated agriculture.
topic: Irrigation Practices, Drainage and Water Management
subject: agriculture
assertion: Groundwater over-extraction in parts of India has caused serious decline of the water table.
reason: Groundwater is an unlimited inexhaustible resource that can never be depleted regardless of pumping rate.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: Groundwater is finite and exhaustible if over-pumped
The reason itself is hydrogeologically false
Declining water tables in Punjab-Haryana are well documented

question_text: Arrange the following components of a typical drip irrigation system from the water source to the crop plant in correct order.
explanation: A drip system starts with a water source (well, reservoir) feeding into a pump. The pressurised water passes through a filtration unit to remove particles that could clog emitters. It then flows through the mainline, sub-mainline and laterals. Finally, emitters on the laterals drip water at each plant root zone.
topic: Irrigation Practices, Drainage and Water Management
subject: agriculture

items.text: Emitters on laterals drip water at each plant root zone
Water source and pump provide pressurised water
Filtration unit removes particles that could clog emitters
Mainline and sub-mainline pipes carry water to laterals

options.text: Q → R → S → P
P → Q → R → S
S → R → Q → P
R → P → Q → S

elimination_hints.hint: Emitters cannot precede the water source
Mainline cannot precede water source and pump
Filtration cannot precede the water source

question_text: Arrange the following irrigation development milestones in India in their correct chronological order from earliest to latest.
explanation: India's canal irrigation history began with the Grand Anicut on the Cauvery built by the Cholas (circa 2nd century CE). The modern era started with the construction of the Upper Ganga Canal completed in 1854 by the British. After independence, the Green Revolution in the 1960s-70s massively expanded tube-well irrigation. PMKSY was launched in 2015 to promote micro-irrigation and 'per drop more crop'.
topic: Irrigation Practices, Drainage and Water Management
subject: agriculture

items.text: PMKSY launched in 2015 for micro-irrigation promotion
Grand Anicut on Cauvery built by Cholas in ancient India
Upper Ganga Canal completed in 1854 in British India

options.text: Q → R → S → P
P → S → R → Q
S → Q → P → R
R → P → Q → S

elimination_hints.hint: 2015 PMKSY cannot be the earliest milestone
1960s Green Revolution cannot precede ancient Chola works
1854 canal cannot precede the ancient Anicut

question_text: State whether the following statement about micro-irrigation adoption in India under PMKSY is TRUE or FALSE with justification.
explanation: The statement is TRUE. Under the 'Per Drop More Crop' component of PMKSY, the Government provides subsidies of up to 55 percent for small and marginal farmers (45 percent for others) to install drip and sprinkler micro-irrigation systems. This has led to significant adoption, with India now having one of the largest micro-irrigated areas in the world.
topic: Irrigation Practices, Drainage and Water Management
subject: agriculture
statement: The Indian Government provides subsidies under PMKSY to promote drip and sprinkler micro-irrigation for farmers.

options.text: TRUE — up to 55% subsidy for small farmers to install drip and sprinkler systems
FALSE — the Government has banned all micro-irrigation in India
FALSE — subsidies are given only for flood irrigation expansion
FALSE — no government scheme supports irrigation in India at all

elimination_hints.hint: India actively promotes micro-irrigation, not bans it
PMKSY promotes water-saving methods, not flood expansion
India has multiple irrigation support schemes

question_text: Study the comparison table of irrigation methods shown in the diagram and answer the question that follows about their characteristics.
explanation: The diagram shows six irrigation methods with their mode of water application, efficiency range and best-suited crop type. Drip irrigation has the highest efficiency at 85-95 percent because water is delivered directly to the root zone through emitters, minimising all major losses. Flood irrigation has the lowest efficiency at 40-50 percent because the entire field surface is wetted.
correct_answer_text: Drip irrigation has the highest efficiency (85-95%) and is best for orchards and spaced crops.
topic: Irrigation Practices, Drainage and Water Management
subject: agriculture

options.text: Flood irrigation has the highest efficiency at 85 to 95 percent
Drip irrigation has the highest efficiency at 85 to 95 percent for orchards
Sprinkler irrigation has the lowest efficiency at 40 to 50 percent
Furrow irrigation is best suited for submerged paddy rice fields

elimination_hints.hint: Flood has the lowest, not highest efficiency
Sprinkler is 65-75%, not the lowest at 40-50%
Paddy uses basin flooding, not furrow
